response:
# Breadcrumb Navigation Accessibility Design Plan > **For Claude:** Use a11y-planner protocol. Review with a11y-critic after implementation. > **Compliance target:** WCAG 2.2 AA > **Users who need accessibility:** Screen reader users (NVDA, VoiceOver), keyboard-only users, low-vision users (focus indicators, contrast), cognitive (clear location signaling) > **Assistive technologies:** NVDA (Windows/Firefox), VoiceOver (macOS/Safari, iOS/Safari), keyboard-only **Feature:** A reusable breadcrumb navigation component that shows the current page's position in the site hierarchy (e.g., Home > Products > Electronics > Laptops), where every item except the last is a link to a parent page and the last item is the static current-page label. **Risk Level:** Low **Component Type:** Navigation landmark / ordered list of links + current-page text --- ## Scope & Context **What is being built?** A breadcrumb trail rendering a linear path from the site root to the current page, with the final item marked as the current location and all preceding items as navigable links. **What user need does it address?** Orientation ("Where am I in this site?") and efficient upward navigation ("Take me back to the Electronics category without using the browser back button repeatedly"). For screen reader users this replaces the visual spatial cue that sighted users get for free. **Who needs accessibility?** - **Screen reader users** — need the trail announced as a navigation region, distinguishable from primary nav, with the current page identified programmatically and the decorative separators silenced. - **Keyboard-only users** — need every link in a logical, predictable Tab order with a visible focus indicator. - **Low-vision users** — need focus indicators and link text that meet contrast minimums, and a layout that survives 200%–400% zoom (relevant because the fixture specifies variable depth up to 5 levels, which can overflow narrow viewports). - **Cognitive** — need the "you are here" signal to be obvious and the link text to be self-describing. **Compliance target:** WCAG 2.2 AA. (No AAA, Section 508, or ADA-specific requirements stated; AA is the default and is the fixture's stated target.) **Assistive technologies to support:** NVDA, VoiceOver, keyboard-only. No JAWS, switch, or voice-control requirement stated — but the design below produces correct results for those too because it relies on native semantics rather than ARIA workarounds. **Risk level — Low, and here is the honest reasoning.** This component has: - No dynamic state (nothing expands, toggles, selects, or loads). - No composite widget interaction (no arrow-key navigation, no roving tabindex, no focus trap). - No overlay, modal, or dynamically inserted content. - No live region need. The entire accessibility surface is: a landmark, an ordered list, native links, one ARIA state attribute (`aria-current`), and hiding decorative separators. The Focus Management and State Communication phases below are genuinely short **because the component genuinely has little of either** — and I am flagging that deliberately rather than padding those sections to look thorough. The two places real defects hide in breadcrumbs are (1) separator handling and (2) the variable-depth/overflow case the fixture mentions but does not fully specify. That is where this plan spends its attention. **Existing code:** None. New component. No `file:line` references to current state because there is no current state. **Constraints (from fixture):** - Variable depth, 2–5 levels. The component must not assume a fixed number of items, and must remain usable at 5 levels on a narrow viewport. - A current-page indicator is required. - Separators are arrow glyphs and are visual-only. **Open question to confirm before implementation (flagged, not assumed):** The fixture says depth is 2–5 levels and the visual example uses arrow separators, but it does not specify whether long trails should **wrap to a second line** or **truncate/collapse** (e.g., "Home > … > Electronics > Laptops"). These two choices have different accessibility consequences (see Visual Accessibility Plan → Reflow, and Content Accessibility Plan → Truncation). My default recommendation is **wrap, do not truncate**, because truncation hides ancestor links from everyone and collapsing them behind a "…" control turns a Low-risk component into a Medium-risk one (it introduces a disclosure widget). If the design team requires truncation, this plan needs a small addendum covering the disclosure pattern — call that out before coding. --- ## Semantic Structure Plan **Landmark region.** The breadcrumb is wrapped in a `
` element with an accessible name. Per the WAI-ARIA APG Breadcrumb pattern, the `nav` carries `aria-label="Breadcrumb"`. This creates a distinct navigation landmark so screen reader users can find it in the landmarks list and so it is not confused with the site's primary navigation. — **WCAG 1.3.1 Info and Relationships**, **WCAG 2.4.1 Bypass Blocks** (provides a named landmark to navigate to/skip), **WCAG 2.4.6 Headings and Labels** (the label describes the region's purpose). - APG reference: https://www.w3.org/WAI/ARIA/apg/patterns/breadcrumb/ **Why `aria-label` and not a visible heading?** A breadcrumb conventionally has no visible "Breadcrumb" heading. `aria-label` on the `nav` gives the landmark its name without adding visible chrome. Note for implementer: do **not** use `title` for this — `title` produces a tooltip, not a reliable accessible name. `aria-label` is the planned mechanism. **List structure.** The trail is an **ordered list** (`
    `), not an unordered list and not a row of `
    `s. Order is meaningful — Home precedes Products precedes Electronics — and `
      ` communicates "this is a sequence of N items, item 1 of 4, item 2 of 4…" to the screen reader. Each crumb is an `
    1. `. — **WCAG 1.3.1 Info and Relationships**. **Within each list item:** - Items 1 through N-1: an `
    2. ` containing an `` with the page name as link text. - Item N (current page): an `
    3. ` containing the page name as **plain text** (not a link), marked with `aria-current="page"`. See Interaction and State sections for the rationale on whether this is a link or text. **Separators.** The arrow glyphs between crumbs are **decorative** and must be invisible to assistive technology. They are NOT list items, NOT inside the link text, and NOT real text content in the accessibility tree. Preferred technique: render each separator as a CSS `::before` on the `
    4. ` (or on the link) and ensure it does not reach the accessibility tree. **Caution flagged:** CSS pseudo-element `content` can be exposed by some screen reader / browser combinations. The safest planned approach is to render the separator as an explicit `` element rather than relying solely on CSS pseudo-element content, OR to use a CSS `::before` whose `content` is paired with the separator's exclusion verified in DOM/AT testing. Either way the testing strategy below requires confirming, in a real screen reader, that "greater than" / "chevron" / "arrow" is **not** announced between crumbs. — **WCAG 1.1.1 Non-text Content** (decorative content has no text alternative / is hidden), **WCAG 1.3.1 Info and Relationships** (separators are presentational, not structural). **Heading hierarchy.** The breadcrumb introduces no headings and must not. It sits inside the page's existing outline (typically between the site header/primary nav and the page's `

      ` / `
      `). It does not contain or affect `h1`–`h6`. **Document outline / placement.** The breadcrumb is placed before `
      ` (or as the first thing inside `
      `, depending on the site template) and after the primary navigation, matching its visual position. DOM order matches visual reading order. — **WCAG 1.3.2 Meaningful Sequence**. **Skip link interaction (note, not a new requirement).** This component does not own the page's skip-to-main-content link, but because it adds a named landmark *before* main content, it slightly lengthens the landmark list. That is acceptable and expected; the existing skip link (a page-level concern per WCAG 2.4.1) continues to let users bypass it. No action required here beyond not breaking landmark order. ### HTML Structure Stub (structure only — not implementation code) ```
      1. Home › separator
      2. Products › separator
      3. Electronics › separator
      4. Laptops ← current page, NOT a link
      ``` Notes embedded in the stub: - The last `
    5. ` has no trailing separator span. - The current page is ``, not ``. (If the team prefers the linked-current variant, see State Communication Design for the alternative; the attribute stays the same, the element changes to ``.) - The separator span is shown inline for clarity; CSS `::before` is an acceptable equivalent provided AT exclusion is verified. --- ## Interaction Pattern Design This component maps to exactly one APG pattern. There are no composite-widget interactions. **APG pattern: Breadcrumb** — https://www.w3.org/WAI/ARIA/apg/patterns/breadcrumb/ The only interactive elements are the ancestor links. They are **native `` elements** and therefore inherit the browser's built-in link semantics and keyboard behavior. We deliberately do **not** add `role`, custom key handlers, or `tabindex` — native links already do the right thing, and overriding them would introduce bugs. **Keyboard interaction model (per native link semantics):** - **Tab** — moves focus to the next link in the trail (left to right). Each ancestor link is a Tab stop. - **Shift+Tab** — moves focus to the previous link. - **Enter** — activates the focused link and navigates to the parent page. (Native `` behavior; Space does **not** activate links, which is correct and expected — do not add a Space handler.) - The current-page item is **not** focusable and not in the Tab order, because it is static text, not a control. This is correct: there is nothing to activate (the user is already on that page). **Screen reader experience (what is announced):** - On entering the region: NVDA/VoiceOver announce the navigation landmark named "Breadcrumb" (e.g., NVDA: "Breadcrumb, navigation region"; VoiceOver: "Breadcrumb, navigation"). - On the list: announced as a list with N items (e.g., "list, 4 items"). - On focusing each ancestor link: the link text and role (e.g., NVDA: "Home, link"; "Products, link"). - On reaching the current page: the page name announced as text with its current state (e.g., VoiceOver: "Laptops, current page"; NVDA reads the text and, when navigating by element, conveys the `aria-current` state). The separators are **not** announced. ### Interactive Elements Table | Widget | APG Pattern | Keyboard | ARIA | WCAG | |--------|-------------|----------|------|------| | Ancestor link (Home, Products, Electronics) | [Breadcrumb](https://www.w3.org/WAI/ARIA/apg/patterns/breadcrumb/) | Tab focuses; Enter activates; Shift+Tab reverses | None added — native `` provides name/role; resolves via link text | 2.1.1 Keyboard, 2.4.4 Link Purpose, 4.1.2 Name Role Value | | Current-page item (Laptops) | [Breadcrumb](https://www.w3.org/WAI/ARIA/apg/patterns/breadcrumb/) | Not focusable (static text) | `aria-current="page"` | 1.3.1 Info and Relationships, 4.1.2 Name Role Value | | Separators (arrows) | [Breadcrumb](https://www.w3.org/WAI/ARIA/apg/patterns/breadcrumb/) | n/a | `aria-hidden="true"` (or CSS pseudo-element excluded from a11y tree) | 1.1.1 Non-text Content | --- ## Focus Management Plan This section is short because the component has no overlays, no modals, no dynamic content insertion, and no composite widget — so there is no focus trap, no focus restoration, and no roving tabindex to design. Padding it would be dead output. What it *does* need: **Tab order (document it step-by-step).** For the example trail Home > Products > Electronics > Laptops: 1. Tab → focus "Home" link. 2. Tab → focus "Products" link. 3. Tab → focus "Electronics" link. 4. Tab → focus moves *out of the breadcrumb* (the "Laptops" current item is text, not a focusable control, so it receives no focus). This order is the DOM order and matches the visual left-to-right, top-to-bottom reading order. — **WCAG 2.4.3 Focus Order**. **Variable depth.** Because depth is 2–5 levels, the number of Tab stops is (depth − 1): a 2-level trail has 1 link, a 5-level trail has 4 links. No special handling — native links and DOM order make this automatic. The current item is never a Tab stop at any depth. **Focus indicator (this is the real focus-management work here).** Every ancestor link must show a clearly visible focus indicator when focused via keyboard. - Use `:focus-visible` (not `:focus`) so the indicator appears for keyboard users without appearing on mouse click. - Use a **two-color / double-ring** indicator — e.g., a dark `outline` plus a light `box-shadow` (or vice versa) — so the indicator stays visible regardless of whether a crumb sits on a light or dark background area of the header. A single-color outline can disappear where the outline color matches the surrounding surface. - The focus indicator must meet **3:1 contrast** against adjacent colors. — **WCAG 2.4.7 Focus Visible**, **WCAG 1.4.11 Non-text Contrast** (focus indicator contrast), **WCAG 2.4.11 Focus Not Obscured (Minimum)** (the focused link must not be hidden behind a sticky header — relevant if the breadcrumb scrolls under a sticky site header). - If breadcrumb links are `display: inline` and wrap across two lines, their focus outline will fragment at the line break. If a focused crumb wraps, prefer `display: inline-block` on the link so the focus ring renders as a clean rectangle. — **WCAG 2.4.7 Focus Visible**. **No focus restoration / no focus trap needed.** Activating a link performs a normal page navigation; the browser handles focus on the destination page. There is no in-page state change to restore focus around. --- ## State Communication Design The only state in this component is "this item is the current page." There are no expanded/collapsed, selected/deselected, pressed, checked, disabled, busy, or invalid states, and no dynamic updates — so there is **no live region** (`aria-live`, `role="status"`, `role="alert"`) in this design, by correct intent rather than omission. Adding one would be wrong. **Current-page state.** - **Visual indicator:** the current item is styled distinctly from the links — typically a different color/weight and, critically, **not** styled to look like a clickable link (no underline-on-hover, no pointer cursor, no link color). It must be visually distinguishable by **more than color alone**: bold weight and/or non-link styling carries the "this is where you are, and it is not clickable" meaning without relying on color. — **WCAG 1.4.1 Use of Color**. - **Programmatic indicator:** `aria-current="page"` on the current item. This is the precise, spec-correct value for "the current page within a set of pages" (as opposed to `aria-current="true"`, `step`, `location`, etc.). — **WCAG 1.3.1 Info and Relationships**, **WCAG 4.1.2 Name, Role, Value**. - Both indicators are present: visual styling AND `aria-current`. Color is never the sole indicator. **Current item as text vs. link — decision and rationale.** Two valid variants exist: 1. **Static text (recommended default, and what the stub uses):** the current item is ``. It is not focusable and not announced as a link. Rationale: the user is already on this page; there is nothing to navigate to. This is the cleaner experience for keyboard and screen reader users and is the more common modern interpretation of the APG example. 2. **Self-link variant:** the current item is ``. Some teams prefer this so the crumb is copyable/consistent. If chosen, it becomes a Tab stop and is announced as "link, current page." This is acceptable but adds a focus stop that lands the user on the page they are already on. The fixture explicitly states the last item "is not a link (static text)," so **Variant 1 is the plan**. I document Variant 2 only so the implementer knows the attribute is identical and the choice is intentional, not accidental. ### State Communication Table | State | Visual | Programmatic | ARIA | WCAG | |-------|--------|--------------|------|------| | Current page (last item) | Distinct color **and** non-link styling/weight; no underline; no pointer cursor; not focusable | Marked as current within the breadcrumb set | `aria-current="page"` | 1.3.1, 1.4.1, 4.1.2 | | Ancestor item (links) | Link styling: underline in content/standalone-link context, link color with 3:1 contrast against surrounding text, pointer cursor, visible `:focus-visible` ring | Native link role + name | (none added — native ``) | 1.4.1, 2.4.4, 2.4.7, 4.1.2 | | Separator | Arrow glyph, decorative | Hidden from a11y tree | `aria-hidden="true"` | 1.1.1 | --- ## Visual Accessibility Plan **Color contrast — WCAG 1.4.3 Contrast (Minimum).** - Ancestor link text: ≥ 4.5:1 against its background (normal text). - Current-page text: ≥ 4.5:1 against its background. (Even though it is "muted" by convention, it must still meet 4.5:1 — a common breadcrumb failure is greying the current item below contrast minimum.) - Separator arrows: these are decorative, so 1.4.3 text contrast does not strictly apply — but if they are the only thing distinguishing items for low-vision users, keep them at ≥ 3:1 as non-text UI for usability. Functionally redundant (the gap and link styling already separate items), so this is best-practice, not a hard AA gate. **Color as sole indicator — WCAG 1.4.1 Use of Color.** Two color-coded distinctions exist; each gets a non-color cue: - *Link vs. current-page:* differentiated by underline + pointer cursor + focus behavior on links, and by weight/non-link styling on the current item — not by color alone. - *Link distinction within surrounding text:* if the breadcrumb sits adjacent to other text, link text must be distinguishable by more than color. In a breadcrumb the links are visually grouped and obviously interactive, but to be safe for the content-link interpretation of 1.4.1, the ancestor link color must have **3:1 contrast against the current-item text color**, and underlining links is the simplest guaranteed-compliant choice. **Font sizing — WCAG 1.4.4 Resize Text.** Use relative units (`rem`/`em`) for font size, never fixed `px`, so the breadcrumb scales with user font-size preferences. Base 16px = 1rem. **Reflow — WCAG 1.4.10 Reflow.** This is the most important visual concern given the variable depth constraint. At 400% zoom (≈320px-equivalent width) a 5-level trail will not fit on one line. The component must **wrap to multiple lines without horizontal scrolling**. Plan: - The `
        ` uses wrapping layout (e.g., `flex-wrap: wrap` or inline flow) so crumbs wrap to the next line. - Container uses `max-width: 100%`; no fixed widths. - Do **not** hide ancestor crumbs at narrow widths to "make it fit" — that removes functionality (the parent links) for small-viewport / high-zoom users, which is itself a 1.4.10 failure of the "no loss of content or functionality" requirement. If the team insists on a compact mode, that is the truncation/disclosure variant flagged in Scope, and it needs its own plan. **Line spacing — WCAG 1.4.8 Visual Presentation (AAA — noted, not required at AA).** Not a strict AA gate, but ensure wrapped crumbs have line-height ≥ 1.5 so a two-line breadcrumb is readable. **Touch target size — WCAG 2.5.8 Target Size (Minimum).** Each ancestor link's clickable/tappable area should be ≥ 24×24 CSS px (the AA 2.5.8 minimum). Because breadcrumb links are small text set close together, this is a real risk: - Ensure adequate spacing/padding so adjacent link targets do not overlap and each has ≥ 24×24px (the 24px AA floor; 44×44px is the AAA 2.5.5 target and a good aim if layout allows). - The separators must not be part of the link target. — **WCAG 2.5.8 Target Size (Minimum)**. **Animation / motion — WCAG 2.3.3.** No animation is planned for this component. If hover transitions are added, respect `prefers-reduced-motion: reduce`. (Noted for completeness; nothing to do unless animation is introduced.) **Color scheme — WCAG 1.4.11 Non-text Contrast.** If the site supports `prefers-color-scheme: dark`, verify all four colors (link text, current text, focus ring, separator) meet contrast in **both** light and dark modes. The focus ring's two-color design helps here. --- ## Content Accessibility Plan **Link text quality — WCAG 2.4.4 Link Purpose (In Context).** Link text is the **page name only** — "Laptops", "Electronics", "Products", "Home" — **not** "Go to Laptops" or "Click here." Rationale and citation: - In a breadcrumb, each link's purpose is clear from its text combined with the surrounding `nav[aria-label="Breadcrumb"]` programmatic context: "Electronics, link" inside a region named "Breadcrumb" unambiguously means "go to the Electronics page." 2.4.4 permits link purpose to be determined from the link text together with its programmatically-determinable context, which the landmark provides. - Prefixing with "Go to" adds verbose, repetitive noise for screen reader users navigating a list of links, with no clarity gain. The fixture's instinct here ("'Laptops' vs 'Go to Laptops'") is correct: **use the bare page name.** - "Home" is acceptable as-is — it is a universally understood breadcrumb root. (Some teams use a house icon for Home; see icon handling below.) **Home as an icon (conditional).** If the Home crumb is rendered as a house **icon** rather than the word "Home": - The icon must have an accessible name. The link needs `aria-label="Home"` (on the ``), and the icon itself (font icon, inline SVG, or ``) must be hidden from AT: `aria-hidden="true"` on the icon element. If using an `` whose link is named via `aria-label`, the `img` `alt` must be **empty** (`alt=""`) so a duplicate description is not read alongside the label. — **WCAG 1.1.1 Non-text Content**, **WCAG 2.4.4 Link Purpose**, **WCAG 4.1.2 Name, Role, Value**. - If Home is rendered as the text word "Home," none of this applies — the text is the accessible name. **Separator content — WCAG 1.1.1.** Covered above: separators are decorative, `aria-hidden="true"` (or excluded CSS pseudo-element), never read. Do not give them alt text or labels. **Current-page text — WCAG 1.3.1 / 4.1.2.** The current item's visible text equals the page title (e.g., "Laptops"). `aria-current="page"` provides the "you are here" semantics; no extra visually-hidden "current page" text is required (and would be redundant with `aria-current`). Do **not** append "(current page)" as visible or hidden text in addition to `aria-current` — that double-announces in screen readers that support `aria-current` (which NVDA and VoiceOver do). **Truncation / overflow content (conditional — tied to the Scope open question).** If the team chooses to truncate long trails: - A "…" collapsed control must be a real, labeled, operable control (e.g., a disclosure button `aria-label="Show all breadcrumb levels"`, `aria-expanded`), **not** a bare "…" text node — otherwise the hidden ancestor links become unreachable for everyone. This converts the component to the Disclosure APG pattern and needs the corresponding plan (focus management on expand, `aria-expanded` state, etc.). **Recommendation stands: wrap instead of truncate to keep this component Low-risk.** **Language — WCAG 3.1.1 / 3.1.2.** Inherits the page's ``. Crumb labels are in the page language. If a specific crumb name is in a different language (rare for category names), wrap it in ``. No action unless multilingual labels occur. **Reading order — WCAG 1.3.2 Meaningful Sequence.** DOM order = root → current = visual left-to-right order. No CSS reordering of crumbs. **Form labels / errors — n/a.** No form, no inputs, no validation in this component. --- ## Testing Strategy **1. Automated testing (axe-core via a11y-test / accessibility-testing).** Run axe-core on the rendered breadcrumb at multiple depths (2, 3, 4, and 5 levels) and in both light and dark mode. axe-core *will* validate: landmark/region usage, `aria-current` value validity, link discernible-text presence, color contrast of link and current text, list structure, `aria-hidden` usage. axe-core *will not* catch the things that actually break breadcrumbs: whether the separator is silently announced by a real screen reader, whether link text is *meaningful* (it can't judge "Laptops" vs "Go to Laptops"), focus-indicator visibility, or whether the current item is wrongly focusable. Those require the manual passes below. Run axe on each depth variant — do not test only the 4-level example. **2. Manual keyboard testing (a11y-test, real key events — `npx playwright test` or agent-browser, never Playwright MCP `browser_press_key`).** - Tab through the trail: focus lands on each ancestor link in left-to-right order. - Confirm the current-page item is **skipped** (receives no focus). - Enter on a focused link navigates to the correct parent URL. - Confirm Shift+Tab reverses the order cleanly. - Focus indicator is clearly visible on every link, at 100% and at 200%/400% zoom, in light and dark mode, and is not clipped when a crumb wraps to a second line. - Confirm focus indicator is not obscured by any sticky header (2.4.11). **3. Screen reader testing (real AT — required, because the highest-value checks here cannot be automated).** - **NVDA + Firefox (Windows):** Region is announced as "Breadcrumb, navigation"; list announced with correct item count; each link announced as "[name], link"; current item announced with its current-page state; **separators are NOT spoken** between crumbs. - **VoiceOver + Safari (macOS):** "Breadcrumb, navigation"; current item announced as "current page"; arrows silent. Also spot-check **VoiceOver + Safari on iOS** (swipe navigation) since this is an e-commerce site with likely heavy mobile traffic — confirm the trail is swipe-navigable and separators are not voiced. - Navigate by landmark (NVDA `D` / VoiceOver rotor) to confirm the breadcrumb appears as a distinct, correctly-named navigation landmark separate from primary nav. **4. DOM verification (MANDATORY — automated + visual inspection are not sufficient).** Inspect the rendered DOM (not the source template) and confirm: - `aria-label="Breadcrumb"` is on the `
        ` element itself. - `aria-current="page"` is on the **current** item and **only** the current item — exactly one per trail, and it lands on the last `
      1. `'s content, not on an ancestor. - Each separator carries `aria-hidden="true"` (or its CSS pseudo-element produces no a11y-tree text node). - The current-page element is **not** an `` and has no `tabindex` making it focusable (Variant 1). - At each depth (2–5), the structure holds and there is exactly one current item and (depth−1) links. This step catches the classic template bug where `aria-current` is applied to the wrong element or to every item, which neither axe nor a screenshot reveals. **5. Visual regression / reflow testing.** - 200% and 400% zoom: the trail wraps without horizontal scroll; no crumb is hidden or cut off; all ancestor links remain present and operable. - 320px-equivalent viewport with a 5-level trail: confirm wrap behavior and that touch targets remain ≥ 24×24px after wrapping. - High-contrast / forced-colors mode (Windows): links, current item, and focus ring remain distinguishable. **6. Acceptance criteria (what "accessible" means for this component):** - Exactly one `
        ` landmark, containing one `
          `. - Exactly (depth−1) focusable links, in correct left-to-right Tab order, each with a visible, contrast-passing `:focus-visible` indicator. - Exactly one item carries `aria-current="page"`; it is the last item; it is not focusable (Variant 1). - Zero separators announced by NVDA or VoiceOver. - Link text is the bare page name; no "Go to"/"Click here" prefixes. - Trail wraps (does not horizontally scroll, does not drop ancestors) at 400% zoom / 320px. - All text ≥ 4.5:1 contrast (including the current item); focus indicator ≥ 3:1; touch targets ≥ 24×24px. - axe-core: zero violations at every depth variant, light and dark. --- ## Implementation Tasks ### Task 1: Breadcrumb component structure + semantics + current-page state 🔍 **Review checkpoint** **Files:** `components/Breadcrumb/Breadcrumb.[tsx|twig|php]`, `components/Breadcrumb/Breadcrumb.[css|scss]`, `components/Breadcrumb/Breadcrumb.test.[ts|js]`, `components/Breadcrumb/Breadcrumb.spec.[js]` (Playwright keyboard/AT) **Structure Stub:** ```
            {for each ancestor crumb 1..N-1}
          1. {pageName} {separatorGlyph}
            2. {current crumb N}
          2. {currentPageName}
          ``` **ARIA Attributes:** - `nav` → `aria-label="Breadcrumb"` — WCAG 1.3.1 Info and Relationships, 2.4.1 Bypass Blocks, 2.4.6 Headings and Labels - current item → `aria-current="page"` — WCAG 1.3.1, 4.1.2 Name Role Value - separator → `aria-hidden="true"` — WCAG 1.1.1 Non-text Content - (Home-as-icon variant only) link → `aria-label="Home"`, icon → `aria-hidden="true"`, `` alt → `""` — WCAG 1.1.1, 2.4.4, 4.1.2 **Keyboard Interactions:** Native links only. Tab/Shift+Tab move between ancestor links; Enter activates. Current item is static text, not focusable, no key handlers. Add no `role`, no `tabindex`, no Space handler. **State / styling logic:** - Render exactly one `aria-current="page"` (last item); render (depth−1) links; render no trailing separator after the last item. - Style current item as non-link (no underline, no pointer cursor) and distinguish by weight as well as color (1.4.1). - `:focus-visible` two-color focus ring on links, ≥ 3:1 (2.4.7, 1.4.11); `display:inline-block` on links so wrapped focus rings don't fragment. - Wrapping layout, `max-width:100%`, relative font units; never drop ancestors at narrow width (1.4.10, 1.4.4). - Link/current text ≥ 4.5:1 (1.4.3); link target ≥ 24×24px (2.5.8); separators outside the link target. **Tests:** - Renders one `nav[aria-label="Breadcrumb"]` > one `ol`. - At depths 2, 3, 4, 5: exactly (depth−1) links and exactly one `aria-current="page"` on the last item. - Tab order is left-to-right across links; current item is not focusable. - Enter on a link navigates to its `href`. - Separators have `aria-hidden="true"` and are not in the accessible name of any link. - DOM verification: `aria-current` on the correct/only element; `aria-label` on the `nav`. - axe-core: zero violations at each depth, light + dark. - Real-AT pass (NVDA + VoiceOver): landmark named, current page announced, separators silent. - Reflow: wraps at 400%/320px, no horizontal scroll, all ancestors present. **WCAG Criteria:** 1.1.1, 1.3.1, 1.3.2, 1.4.1, 1.4.3, 1.4.4, 1.4.10, 1.4.11, 2.1.1, 2.4.1, 2.4.3, 2.4.4, 2.4.6, 2.4.7, 2.4.11, 2.5.8, 4.1.2 **a11y-critic checkpoint 🔍:** Verify (1) APG Breadcrumb pattern followed; (2) `nav[aria-label="Breadcrumb"]` landmark present and correctly named; (3) `
            `/`
          1. ` semantics, not divs; (4) `aria-current="page"` on exactly one item — the last — and that item is non-focusable text (or self-link if Variant 2 was chosen intentionally); (5) separators provably hidden from AT (not just visually) — check real screen reader output, not just `aria-hidden` presence; (6) link text is bare page names, no "Go to"/"click here"; (7) focus indicator visible, two-color, contrast-passing, un-fragmented on wrap; (8) reflow keeps all ancestor links operable at 400% zoom; (9) no live region was added (correct absence); (10) DOM-level confirmation that attributes landed on the right elements. --- ## a11y-Critic Review Checkpoints | Checkpoint | After Task | Focus | |-----------|-----------|-------| | 🔍 1 | Task 1 | APG Breadcrumb conformance; named `nav` landmark; `ol`/`li` semantics; single correct `aria-current="page"` on non-focusable last item; separators hidden from AT (verified in real SR, not just attribute presence); bare-page-name link text; visible two-color focus indicator; reflow preserves all ancestor links; DOM verification of attribute placement | --- ### Contract Appendix (for spec-kitty-bridge WP translation) ### Architecture Overview Single presentational navigation component. Semantic approach: a named `
            ` landmark wrapping an `
              ` of `
            1. ` items; ancestor items are native `` links; the current item is static `` text; arrow separators are `aria-hidden="true"` (or CSS pseudo-elements excluded from the a11y tree). ARIA pattern choice: WAI-ARIA APG **Breadcrumb** — minimal ARIA (`aria-label`, `aria-current`, `aria-hidden`) layered over native HTML semantics; no custom roles, no `tabindex`, no key handlers. Focus management strategy: rely entirely on native link Tab order (no traps, no restoration, no roving tabindex); the only focus work is a visible, contrast-passing, two-color `:focus-visible` indicator that survives wrapping. State strategy: one state (`current page`) expressed both visually (non-link styling + weight, not color alone) and programmatically (`aria-current="page"`); no live regions because there is no dynamic content. Layout strategy: wrapping (not truncating) layout so variable 2–5 level depth reflows without horizontal scroll and without dropping ancestor links at high zoom / narrow viewports. ### Implementation Tasks #### Task 1: Breadcrumb component structure + semantics + current-page state Estimated Effort: low Depends on: none Reasoning for estimate: one new presentational component, no state management, no interaction logic beyond native links, single ARIA state attribute. The only non-trivial work is the focus-indicator CSS (two-color, wrap-safe) and the reflow/wrap layout — both contained, well-understood CSS. No backend, no data layer, no migration. #### Test Strategy for Task 1 Unit/DOM tests: structure (`nav[aria-label]` > `ol` > `li`); per-depth correctness at depths 2–5 (exactly depth−1 links, exactly one `aria-current="page"` on the last item, no trailing separator); current item not focusable; separators `aria-hidden`. axe-core scans at each depth in light and dark mode (zero violations). Real-keyboard Playwright (`.spec.js`, CDP-backed): Tab order, current item skipped, Enter navigation, focus-indicator visibility at 100/200/400% zoom and on wrap. Real-AT manual passes: NVDA+Firefox and VoiceOver+Safari (macOS and iOS) — landmark naming, current-page announcement, separator silence. Mandatory DOM-inspection step on rendered output confirming `aria-current`/`aria-label`/`aria-hidden` land on the correct elements and that exactly one current item exists. Reflow tests at 400% zoom / 320px confirming wrap with no horizontal scroll and no dropped ancestors. #### Acceptance Criteria for Task 1 - One `
              ` landmark containing one `
                `; items are `
              1. `; ancestors are ``, current is static text. (1.3.1, 2.4.6) - Exactly (depth−1) focusable links in left-to-right Tab order; current item not focusable; Enter activates links. (2.1.1, 2.4.3) - Exactly one `aria-current="page"`, on the last item only. (1.3.1, 4.1.2) - Zero separators announced by NVDA or VoiceOver; separators carry `aria-hidden="true"`. (1.1.1) - Link text is the bare page name (no "Go to"/"click here"); link purpose clear from text + Breadcrumb landmark context. (2.4.4) - Visible two-color `:focus-visible` indicator on links, ≥ 3:1 contrast, un-fragmented on wrap, not obscured by sticky header. (2.4.7, 1.4.11, 2.4.11) - Current and link text ≥ 4.5:1 contrast; focus ring ≥ 3:1; touch targets ≥ 24×24px. (1.4.3, 1.4.11, 2.5.8) - Trail wraps (no horizontal scroll, no dropped ancestor links) at 400% zoom / 320px equivalent. (1.4.10, 1.4.4) - No live region present (correct absence). - axe-core: zero violations at every depth, light and dark. ### Failure Modes - **Separator leaks to AT:** arrow glyph announced ("greater than", "chevron", "right-pointing arrow") between every crumb because it was placed in real text content or a CSS pseudo-element that the browser/SR exposes. Mitigation: `aria-hidden` span or AT-verified pseudo-element; mandatory real-SR test. - **`aria-current` misplacement:** applied to every item, to an ancestor, or to none — template loop bug. Detectable only via DOM inspection (not axe, not screenshots). Mitigation: per-depth DOM-verification test asserting exactly one, on the last item. - **Current item wrongly focusable / styled as a link:** rendered as `` or given `tabindex`, adding a dead Tab stop and implying the current page is clickable. Mitigation: assert current item is not an `` and not focusable (Variant 1). - **Color-only current-page distinction:** current item differentiated from links by color alone (1.4.1 failure), often also failing 4.5:1 because it's greyed out. Mitigation: distinguish by weight/non-link styling and verify contrast. - **Invisible / fragmented focus indicator:** single-color outline disappears against the header surface, or `:focus` (not `:focus-visible`) shows rings on mouse click, or `display:inline` fragments the ring across a line wrap. Mitigation: two-color ring, `:focus-visible`, `display:inline-block`. - **Reflow failure on deep trails:** 5-level trail forces horizontal scroll at high zoom, or the design "solves" overflow by hiding ancestor links — removing functionality for narrow-viewport/high-zoom users (1.4.10 failure). Mitigation: wrapping layout; never drop ancestors; if truncation is mandated, escalate to a Disclosure-pattern addendum (raises risk from Low to Medium). - **Verbose link text:** "Go to Laptops" / "Click here" added under a mistaken belief it aids clarity, producing repetitive screen-reader output. Mitigation: bare page names; rely on the Breadcrumb landmark for context (2.4.4). - **Redundant current-page announcement:** appending visible/hidden "(current page)" text *in addition to* `aria-current`, double-announcing in NVDA/VoiceOver. Mitigation: rely on `aria-current` alone for the state. - **Touch targets too small:** small breadcrumb links set tightly together fall under 24×24px and/or overlap, and separators are accidentally tappable as part of the link. Mitigation: padding/spacing to ≥ 24×24px; exclude separators from the link target. ---
